mission.title: woodpecker-ci: Woodpecker 2.4 breaks privileged steps/plugins with Kubernetes backend
mission.description: Woodpecker 2.4 breaks privileged steps/plugins with Kubernetes backend. Community-reported issue.

steps.title: Identify woodpecker-ci troubleshoot symptoms
steps.description:
Check for the issue in your woodpecker-ci deployment:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n woodpecker-ci -l app.kubernetes.io/name=woodpecker-ci
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=woodpecker-ci -n woodpecker-ci --tail=100 | grep -i error
```
Look for errors or warnings in the logs that may indicate the issue.

steps.title: Review woodpecker-ci configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant woodpecker-ci configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n woodpecker-ci -l app.kubernetes.io/name=woodpecker-ci
kubectl get configmap -n woodpecker-ci -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=woodpecker-ci
```
This worked with WP 2.3 and Kubernetes backend:

```
  publish:
    image: woodpeckerci/plugin-docker-buildx
    settings:
      repo: *repo
      tags: 8h
```

Using WP 2.4 the docker daemon does not start.

steps.title: Apply the fix for Woodpecker 2.4 breaks privileged steps/plugins with…
steps.description:
We also have to support this for other backends and as for example docker would allow mounting the docker.sock again, you could reach root access by using volumes. https://github.com/woodpecker-ci/woodpecker/discussions/2272 might be an option.
```yaml
publish:
    image: woodpeckerci/plugin-docker-buildx
    settings:
      repo: *repo
      tags: 8h
```

steps.title: Confirm Woodpecker 2.4 breaks privileged steps/plugins… is resolved
steps.description:
Verify the fix by checking that the original error no longer occurs:
```bash
kubectl logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=woodpecker-ci -n woodpecker-ci --tail=50 --since=5m
kubectl get events -n woodpecker-ci --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm that the issue symptoms are gone.

resolution.summary: We also have to support this for other backends and as for example docker would allow mounting the docker.sock again, you could reach root access by using volumes. https://github.com/woodpecker-ci/woodpecker/discussions/2272 might be an option.
codeSnippets:
publish:
    image: woodpeckerci/plugin-docker-buildx
    settings:
      repo: *repo
      tags: 8h

publish:
    image: woodpeckerci/plugin-docker-buildx
    privileged: true
    backend_options:
      kubernetes:
        securityContext:
          privileged: true
    settings:
      repo: *repo
      tags: 8h
      daemon.debug: "true"

privileged: true
    backend_options:
      kubernetes:
        securityContext:
          privileged: true

prerequisites.description: A running Kubernetes cluster with woodpecker-ci installed or the issue environment reproducible.
